preFix.title: Installing external wall insulation (EWI/ETICS) on a solid masonry house
preFix.subtitle: Preparing, fixing and rendering an external insulation system
preFix.excerpt: External wall insulation transforms the thermal performance of solid-walled houses that can't accept cavity fill. A 90 mm expanded polystyrene or mineral-wool board system on a 1900s brick terrace brings the wall U-value from around 2.1 down to 0.27 W/m2K, saving roughly 3,500 kWh per year in a typical semi. The job requires scaffolding and a registered installer for the basecoat-and-render phase, but understanding the system helps you spec, supervise, and commission correctly.
preFix.sourceNotes: INCA (Insulated Render and Cladding Association) EWI guidance 2024. BS EN 13499 and BS EN 13500 for polystyrene and mineral wool EWI systems. gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B 2022 for 0.30 W/m2K solid-wall target. PAS 2035:2023 whole-house retrofit standard.

content.text: Solid masonry walls (Victorian and Edwardian brick terraces, pre-1919 stone cottages, early-twentieth-century concrete-block semis) cannot accept cavity fill because there is no cavity. External wall insulation is the only option that avoids the disruption and condensation risk of internal dry-lining. A 90 mm EPS board system on a 225 mm solid brick wall brings the
U-value
from roughly 2.1 to 0.27 W/m2K, close to the Part L target of 0.30. On a 1930s three-bedroom semi with 90 m2 of exposed wall, that is around 3,500 kWh saved per year.

EWI installation requires scaffolding, specialist adhesives and renders, and mechanical fixings that must be specified for the substrate. The render phase is system-specific and voids the product warranty if applied by an uncertified operative. If public funding (ECO4, Great British Insulation Scheme) is being used,
PAS 2035
mandates a certified Retrofit Coordinator and a registered installer. This tutorial covers specification, supervision, and commissioning — not DIY installation.
Choosing the board type
Two board types dominate residential
ETICS
installations:
Expanded polystyrene (EPS): lambda 0.033 to 0.038 W/mK, lowest cost, most common. A 90 mm board gives a U-value of around 0.27 for a 225 mm brick wall. Needs a render finish; cannot be left exposed.

Mineral wool (stone wool): lambda 0.034 to 0.040 W/mK, slightly higher cost, fire-rated A1 or A2, diffusion-open so the wall can dry outward. Preferred in higher-rise buildings and on walls with existing damp problems.

For a standard two-storey domestic wall, EPS is the cost-effective default. For a wall with active salt or moisture movement, mineral wool is the safer choice. Both systems are installed with the same basecoat-and-mesh approach.
Planning and permitted development

EWI typically adds 100 to 150 mm to the outer face of the wall, which can push a house closer to the boundary. In most cases EWI on a principal or side elevation of a house falls within permitted development in England (Part 1, Class A, provided the materials match the existing appearance in a conservation area). In Article 4 areas, conservation areas, or on listed buildings, full planning permission is required. Check with the local planning authority before committing to specification.
What you need to know before the installer arrives
Wall condition. Any spalled bricks, open pointing, or active water ingress must be repaired before the boards go on. Photograph all defects before scaffold erection.

Services. Meter boxes, gas pipes, electrical conduit, and external lights must be moved outward or replaced with extension pieces before the boards are fitted. Agree the location and cost of each service deviation in the specification.

Reveals and heads. The window reveals (the side returns into each opening) must be insulated and rendered too. Thinner boards (50 to 60 mm) are used here to avoid reducing the window opening. If the windows are to be replaced within five years, sequence that work after the EWI so the frames sit in the new plane.

Ventilation. Adding 90 mm of EWI and rendering it airtight increases the airtightness of the building. Before sign-off, confirm that background ventilators (trickle vents in window frames or wall vents) are present and open, and that any extraction fans (bathroom, kitchen) vent to outside.
The installation sequence

Substrate preparation. The installer cleans the wall, fills holes, re-points loose mortar, and applies primer where the wall is highly absorbent. Efflorescence (white salt crystals) is treated with a consolidant. Mould is killed with a fungicidal wash, then brushed off.

Starter track. A galvanised aluminium starter track is fixed to the wall at the base of the board zone, typically 150 mm above finished ground level. The track holds the first course of boards off the ground and carries the weight of the system while the adhesive sets.

Board adhesion and fixing. Adhesive mortar is applied to the back of each board (either a full-coverage trowel bed or the manufacturer's specified dab pattern). Boards are pressed onto the wall and the joints are staggered (brick-bond pattern) with no continuous vertical joints. Mechanical fixings (expanding plastic plugs with large washers) are driven through the boards into the masonry after the adhesive has cured. The number of fixings per board is specified by the system supplier based on wind load zone.
Board rasping and filling. Any high spots are rasped flat. Joints and plug heads are filled with adhesive mortar. The face must be flat to within 3 mm over a 2 m straight-edge before the
basecoat and mesh
is applied.

Basecoat, mesh, and second pass. The first basecoat layer (2 to 3 mm) is trowelled on, the alkali-resistant glass-fibre mesh is pressed in and lapped at all joints by a minimum of 100 mm, and a second coat brings the total to 4 to 6 mm. Corner beads and movement beads are bedded into the mesh layer.

Primer and finish render. After the basecoat has set (minimum 3 days at 10°C; follow the product data sheet for exact timing), apply primer. Put on the silicone or acrylic finish render in a single wet-on-wet pass. Working wet-on-wet prevents visible joints in the finished surface.
Commissioning checks
Walk the completed installation before the scaffold comes down. Check:
All reveals are insulated and rendered, with no exposed board edges.
Movement joints at every floor level and at changes in substrate type (brick to block, new extension to original wall).
Starter track sits 150 mm above ground; no boards buried in soft landscaping.
All service penetrations (gas pipes, meter cabinets, waste pipes) have been sealed with flexible sealant and finished with a neat collar or beading.

No cracking in the render face. A few hairline cracks in the finish coat are normal curing shrinkage; cracks that follow the board joints indicate the mesh was not lapped, which is a defect requiring remediation.
What it looks like done well versus done badly

Done well: uniform render texture, no visible joints, clean movement beads at every floor and corner, insulated reveals, and sealed service penetrations. In cold weather, a thermal camera shows the wall cooling evenly, with small cold spots only at fixings and window frames. This is the expected
thermal bridging
pattern for a correctly installed system.

Done badly: boards break joint at corners and window reveals. The starter track is buried in mulch. There are no movement joints. Window reveals are left uninsulated, which shows as a cold stripe on a thermal image. Service penetrations are filled with expanding foam, which prises the render away within two winters.

attrs.fix: Cut out the render over the joint, add a strip of mesh with 100 mm laps each side, re-render and prime
attrs.cause: Mesh laps too short, or no mesh over board joints
attrs.symptom: Cracks following board joint lines appearing within a year

attrs.fix: Cut back ground level or dig a French drain alongside the system; add a render stop bead and sealant at the base
attrs.cause: Ground level within 150 mm of the board base, or no DPC extension fitted
attrs.symptom: Damp patch appearing at base of EWI system after rain

attrs.fix: Locate extent of delamination by tapping; open, re-adhere, and re-render. Notify installer — this is a warranty defect if discovered within the guarantee period
attrs.cause: Board has delaminated from the wall (failed adhesive or substrate prep)
attrs.symptom: Hollow tap sound when knocking on the render face

attrs.fix: Open trickle vents fully; fit or upgrade bathroom and kitchen extraction; consider a whole-house ventilation assessment
attrs.cause: Ventilation not checked before installation; the building is now tighter but background ventilation was not upgraded
attrs.symptom: Condensation appearing inside on walls that were previously dry
